finding.summary: The acc_ctrl_o output of acct_wrapper is computed by OR-ing acct_mem entries with {8{we_flag}}, unconditionally forcing the lower 8 bits of the first access-control word to all-ones whenever we_flag is asserted, regardless of any reglk_ctrl lock or privilege check. This means any entity that can assert we_flag can grant itself (and all downstream consumers of acc_ctrl_o/acc_ctrl_c) maximum access permissions for peripheral 0, bypassing the register-lock protection that governs all other writes.

evidence.description: Line 49: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};` unconditionally ORs all 8 bits of the we_flag signal into the lowest access-control word. When we_flag=1, acc_ctrl_o[7:0] becomes 0xFF regardless of what is stored in acct_mem[0] or whether reglk_ctrl locks are active.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag bypass forces maximum access permissions on acc_ctrl_o without any lock or privilege check

evidence.description: Lines 90-108 show that writes to acct_mem entries are guarded by reglk_ctrl bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[13], reglk_ctrl[1], reglk_ctrl[7]). The lock protection on writes is present, but the we_flag path in line 49 completely circumvents this protection for acct_mem[0] by OR-forcing all bits high in the output, without checking any lock bit.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl write guards are bypassed by the we_flag OR path on the output assignment

evidence.description: Lines 215-224: acc_ctrl_c is derived directly from acc_ctrl (which is driven by acc_ctrl_o from acct_wrapper) and is distributed to all peripherals including pkt_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, and dma_wrapper as their acct_ctrl_i. Corrupting acc_ctrl_o[7:0] via we_flag propagates to all peripheral enable gates.
evidence.supports_claim: acc_ctrl_o feeds acc_ctrl_c which gates enable signals for all security-critical peripherals

evidence.description: Line 95: `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i;` in dma_wrapper — if acct_ctrl_i is forced to 1 by the we_flag bypass, DMA peripheral access is unconditionally enabled regardless of privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: Forcing acc_ctrl_c high enables DMA operations without privilege or lock checks

evidence.description: Line 68: `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i;` in pkt_wrapper — same pattern; pkt_wrapper (which handles fuse/key material) is unconditionally enabled if acct_ctrl_i is forced high.
evidence.supports_claim: Forcing acc_ctrl_c high enables access to sensitive fuse/key registers in pkt_wrapper

evidence.description: Line 72: `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i;` in reglk_wrapper — forcing acct_ctrl_i high enables writes to the register-lock controller itself, potentially unlocking all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Forcing acc_ctrl_c high enables writes to the register-lock controller, defeating the entire lock hierarchy

evidence.description: Line 516-517: The commented-out version `//assign rom_req = rom_req_acct;` was replaced with `assign rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0];`. If acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0] is forced to 1 via we_flag, the bootrom access check is trivially satisfied for any privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: Bootrom access control is defeated if acc_ctrl_c[*][0] is forced high by the we_flag bypass

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module computes acc_ctrl_o by OR-ing we_flag (replicated 8 times) directly into the lowest access-control word on the output path (line 49). This is a combinational bypass that is not conditioned on any reglk_ctrl lock bit or privilege level. The acc_ctrl_o output feeds riscv_peripherals.acc_ctrl, which is reorganized into acc_ctrl_c and distributed as acct_ctrl_i to every security-critical peripheral (pkt_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, dma_wrapper) and used to gate bootrom access. Any entity able to assert we_flag=1 can force maximum access permissions across all peripherals, bypassing the register-lock (reglk_ctrl) write protections that are otherwise carefully enforced per-entry in the always block.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured peripheral that can assert we_flag gains unconditional read/write access to all security peripherals including: the register-lock controller (allowing full unlock), the DMA engine (enabling arbitrary memory transfers), the fuse/key store (pkt_wrapper), and the bootrom. This effectively collapses the entire peripheral access-control hierarchy to a single unguarded signal.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of we_flag (riscv_peripherals.we_flag_0) is referenced in the graph (LOC-0008) but its computation logic is not present in the provided snippets. If we_flag is itself strongly privilege-gated or can only be asserted in a legitimate supervisor context, the severity is reduced. However, the absence of any lock check on the output OR path is a structural violation regardless of we_flag's source.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper reset condition initializes acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), which is the permissive/allow-all state, meaning a reset event grants full access to all peripherals rather than a deny-all default.

evidence.description: On reset (~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. This is the all-ones permissive state used as access control values.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes access control memory to fully permissive (all-ones) rather than deny-all (all-zeros), creating a window where all access is granted after any reset event.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem: assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. When acct_mem is 0xFFFFFFFF after reset, acc_ctrl_o becomes all-ones, granting access to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control output distributed to all peripherals reflects the permissive reset state of acct_mem.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed as acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i]). When acc_ctrl (derived from acct_mem after reset) is all-ones, acc_ctrl_c will also be all-ones for all peripherals and privilege levels.
evidence.supports_claim: All-ones in acct_mem propagates to acc_ctrl_c, which gates access to every peripheral including bootrom, DMA, pkt, and reglk wrappers.

evidence.description: rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]. If acc_ctrl_c is all-ones after reset, bootrom access is gated only by rom_req_acct with no privilege restriction.
evidence.supports_claim: All-ones reset value removes effective privilege-level gating for bootrom access immediately after reset.

evidence.description: DMA wrapper write logic at line 129 uses 'else if(en && we)' with no reglk_ctrl guard on several DMA registers (start_reg, length_reg, address registers). The en signal depends on acct_ctrl_i which after reset is all-ones permissive. DMA registers including source/dest addresses can be written freely.
evidence.supports_claim: After reset, with acct_mem all-ones propagating through acc_ctrl_c to acct_ctrl_i, DMA control registers are writable without effective access restriction.

evidence.description: The reset block sets acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff rather than 0 for all j. This is architecturally backwards for a security access control table: fail-open on reset instead of fail-secure (deny-all).
evidence.supports_claim: The insecure reset default (all-ones = permit) means any reset, including adversarially triggered resets, opens all access gates system-wide until software re-programs the table.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module maintains acct_mem, which is the system-wide access control table distributed to all peripherals (bootrom, DMA, PKT, REGLK) as acc_ctrl_o/acc_ctrl_c. On every reset event (rst_ni deasserted or rst_6 asserted), acct_mem is set to 32'hffffffff for every entry. This all-ones value propagates through acc_ctrl_o to acc_ctrl_c and is then distributed as acct_ctrl_i to all peripheral wrappers. The result is a fully permissive access control state immediately after reset. For the bootrom, this means acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0] is 1 for all privilege levels, removing effective privilege isolation. For the DMA, acct_ctrl_i=1 enables en (en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i), allowing DMA register writes. For the REGLK and PKT wrappers, the same permissive enable applies. A secure design would reset to all-zeros (deny-all) and require explicit software initialization to grant access.
finding.security_impact: Any reset event (power-on, watchdog, software-triggered, or adversarially triggered rst_6) places the entire peripheral access control system in a fully permissive state. During this window, code running at any privilege level can access the bootrom, write DMA source/destination addresses and initiate transfers, and modify register lock values. An attacker who can trigger a reset while retaining code execution can exploit this window to escalate privileges, exfiltrate data via DMA, or reconfigure the lock registers before the firmware re-initializes the access control table.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact duration of the permissive window depends on firmware initialization time, which is not shown in the RTL. It is also possible that rst_6 is intended as a full-system reset that always precedes secure boot, but the code comment-out history (line 516 shows a previously commented-out version of the rom_req assignment) suggests this logic was actively changed, indicating this is a real design concern rather than an intended behavior.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The provided source snippet shows only module port declarations for the acct_wrapper module (clock, reset, register lock input, AXI request/response ports, access control output, and an internal memory register). There is no concrete evidence of a missing guard, bypassed lock/auth check, unsafe reset or debug behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update within this snippet. The chain contains a single isolated signal node with no edges, and the snippet does not show any logic that processes or enforces (or fails to enforce) permissions. Insufficient context exists to identify a permission-related vulnerability.

finding.summary: AXI protection field (aw.prot and ar.prot) is hardwired to zero when driving the ACCT (Access Control) wrapper, stripping all AXI privilege/security/instruction-type information from every transaction reaching the access-control peripheral.

evidence.description: Lines 1785 and 1796 unconditionally assign acct_axi_req.aw.prot and acct_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (all-zero). The AXI AxPROT field encodes privilege level (bit 0), secure/non-secure world (bit 1), and instruction/data access (bit 2). Forcing all bits to zero means every transaction to the ACCT module is presented as unprivileged, secure, data-access regardless of the actual requester's privilege or security state.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI protection attribute that should carry privilege and security-world information to the access-control peripheral is permanently zeroed out, so the ACCT module cannot make access-control decisions based on requester privilege or TrustZone secure/non-secure state.

evidence.description: The acct_wrapper instance is the Access Control Table peripheral responsible for generating acc_ctrl_o signals that govern peripheral access. It receives acct_axi_req as its AXI request input, meaning the zeroed prot field directly affects what the ACCT module sees for every configuration transaction.
evidence.supports_claim: The resource being misconfigured is the access-control enforcement module itself; erasing privilege information from its AXI port undermines the integrity of the whole permission chain.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper declares axi_req_i (ariane_axi::req_t) as its AXI slave port. The prot field of this full AXI-4 type is meaningful and expected to carry requester privilege/security attributes. The wrapper also accepts reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i, indicating an intent to enforce access policy, but the incoming prot information is already destroyed before it arrives.
evidence.supports_claim: The entry point for access-control configuration transactions arrives with its protection metadata zeroed, defeating any privilege-level gating the ACCT module might attempt.

finding.reasoning_summary: The riscv_peripherals module builds acct_axi_req by tying off the non-AXI-lite fields. Both the write-channel protection field (aw.prot) and the read-channel protection field (ar.prot) are assigned a constant '0. This means the ACCT peripheral, which is responsible for producing access-control outputs (acc_ctrl_o) governing other peripherals, always observes an unprivileged, secure, data-access requester irrespective of the true privilege level or security world of the originating master. An attacker or unprivileged software that can reach the ACCT AXI slave address can program the access-control table while appearing to the hardware as a trusted privileged requester, and conversely a legitimate privileged requester cannot be distinguished from an unprivileged one.
finding.security_impact: Any software that can issue AXI transactions to the ACCT address range can modify the access-control table without privilege or security-world enforcement. Because the ACCT table governs which masters may access which peripherals (acc_ctrl_o), a successful write by unprivileged or untrusted software can grant itself or deny others peripheral access, completely bypassing the intended hardware-enforced permission model.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal logic of acct_wrapper that consumes aw.prot/ar.prot is not present in the provided snippets, so it is theoretically possible the module ignores prot entirely. However, the pattern of deliberately tying off AXI-lite bridge signals while leaving prot=0 on a full AXI port connected to a security-enforcement peripheral is itself a clear design flaw regardless of whether acct_wrapper currently checks prot, because it precludes any future or intended privilege-level enforcement.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The provided source snippets show only the port declaration of axi_resp_o in acct_wrapper.sv and the instantiation of i_acct_wrapper in riscv_peripherals.sv, including the reglk_ctrl_i connection. There is no concrete source-code evidence of a missing guard, bypassed lock check, unsafe reset/debug behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update. The snippets confirm that reglk_ctrl is passed in (line 1728) and that the module has a register lock input, but no logic showing how axi_resp_o is driven or whether it bypasses any access control check is present in the provided snippets. Without evidence of a specific violation in the available code, a finding cannot be substantiated.

finding.summary: The DMA wrapper (dma_wrapper) register file lacks any reglk_ctrl-based write protection on its registers, allowing unprivileged writes to DMA control registers (start, length, source/destination addresses, etc.) even when register lock bits should be enforced.

evidence.description: The DMA write-side always block writes registers (start_reg, length_reg, source_addr_lsb_reg, source_addr_msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb_reg, dest_addr_msb_reg, done_reg, core_lock_reg, end_reg) based solely on 'en && we' with no check against reglk_ctrl_i. Unlike acct_wrapper which checks reglk_ctrl[N] before each write, dma_wrapper performs no such guard.
evidence.supports_claim: DMA registers can be written without any register-lock protection, bypassing the reglk_ctrl mechanism that is applied in other wrappers.

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, each acct_mem entry write is guarded by a reglk_ctrl bit check (e.g., 'acct_mem[00] <= reglk_ctrl[5] ? acct_mem[00] : wdata'), demonstrating the intended pattern where reglk_ctrl bits protect writes to sensitive registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The design intent is for reglk_ctrl bits to block writes to protected registers; acct_wrapper correctly implements this, but dma_wrapper does not.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl[8*8+7:8*8] is passed to dma_wrapper as reglk_ctrl_i, confirming the lock bits are wired to the DMA wrapper but the wrapper never uses them for write protection.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl bits are delivered to dma_wrapper but are not used to gate writes, making the lock mechanism ineffective for DMA registers.

evidence.description: dma_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i as an input but the write-side logic (SNIP-0011) never references it to protect any register write, confirming the missing guard.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl_i port is present but unused in the write protection path of dma_wrapper.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper read-side logic checks reglk_ctrl_i bits before returning sensitive data (pkey_loc, fuse_rdata), showing the intended lock enforcement pattern is used in reads for pkt_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: Other wrappers use reglk_ctrl_i to protect sensitive register accesses; dma_wrapper omits equivalent write guards.

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_ctrl mechanism is designed to lock peripheral registers against unauthorized writes and reads. In acct_wrapper, every write to acct_mem is conditioned on the corresponding reglk_ctrl bit being clear. In pkt_wrapper, reads of sensitive fields check reglk_ctrl_i bits. However, in dma_wrapper, the write-side always block (lines 115-152) unconditionally writes all DMA control registers (start, length, source/destination addresses, core_lock, done, end) whenever 'en && we' is asserted, with no reglk_ctrl_i guard at all. Although reglk_ctrl[8*8+7:8*8] is wired to dma_wrapper.reglk_ctrl_i, the signal is never consulted in the write path, making the register lock ineffective for the DMA peripheral.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can issue AXI write transactions to the DMA address space can overwrite DMA start, length, source address, destination address, and control registers regardless of the register lock state. This could allow unauthorized DMA transfers, memory corruption, or denial of service by redirecting DMA operations to arbitrary memory regions.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The dma_wrapper read-side logic (after line 157) is not shown in the provided snippets, so it is uncertain whether read protection via reglk_ctrl_i is also absent. Additionally, acct_ctrl_i gating of 'en' in acct_wrapper (line 73) provides an additional access control layer, but whether dma_wrapper applies similar 'en' gating is not fully visible; however, the absence of any reglk_ctrl check in the write case statements is clear from the available source.

finding.summary: In reglk_wrapper, address case 2 of the write path incorrectly reads reglk_mem[3] (instead of reglk_mem[2]) as the retained value when the lock bit is set, corrupting reglk_mem[2] with the wrong register content and preventing proper lock enforcement.

evidence.description: At line 93, the write case for address 2 assigns `reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;`. When the lock bit reglk_ctrl[1] is set, the intent is to preserve the existing value of reglk_mem[2], but instead it loads reglk_mem[3] into reglk_mem[2]. This is an incorrect protection state update: rather than holding reglk_mem[2] unchanged, it overwrites it with the value from a different register slot.
evidence.supports_claim: The locked-register preservation logic is broken for reglk_mem[2]: a locked write silently replaces reglk_mem[2] with reglk_mem[3], corrupting the lock state that controls downstream peripherals.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o is assembled as {reglk_mem[5],...,reglk_mem[0]} and distributed to all peripheral wrappers (acct_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, dma_wrapper). A corrupted reglk_mem[2] propagates incorrect lock bits to those peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem[2] is part of the reglk_ctrl_o bus that enforces read/write locks on all protected peripherals; corruption of this value undermines the entire register-lock security chain.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl bits (derived from reglk_mem via reglk_ctrl_o) to gate both writes (e.g. reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[13], reglk_ctrl[1], reglk_ctrl[7]) and reads (reglk_ctrl[4], reglk_ctrl[2], reglk_ctrl[0], reglk_ctrl[6]) on acct_mem entries. If reglk_mem[2] is corrupted, the corresponding lock bits become incorrect.
evidence.supports_claim: Access-control memory in acct_wrapper is protected by lock bits sourced from reglk_mem[2]; a corrupted value can either fail to block unauthorized access or incorrectly block legitimate access.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper gates readback of sensitive pkey_loc and fuse_rdata on reglk_ctrl_i bits (lines 103, 105, 107). These bits originate from reglk_mem via reglk_ctrl_o, so corruption of reglk_mem[2] can expose or incorrectly suppress sensitive key/fuse data.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive cryptographic key location and fuse read data in pkt_wrapper are gated by lock bits that can be corrupted due to the reglk_mem[2] bug.

evidence.description: The write guard for reglk_mem uses `reglk_ctrl[1]` as the lock predicate for entries 1-5. For entry 2, the guard body mistakenly references reglk_mem[3] rather than reglk_mem[2] as the hold value, so the guard does not correctly preserve the protected state.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard logic at line 93 is defective: it does not correctly implement the 'hold current value when locked' semantic.

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module maintains reglk_mem[], which is concatenated into reglk_ctrl_o and broadcast to all security-sensitive peripherals as register-lock control. The write always-block at line 93 (SNIP-0013) contains a copy-paste error: `reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata` — when the lock bit is asserted, the register is supposed to retain its own value, but instead it is overwritten with the value of reglk_mem[3]. This means every locked write to slot 2 silently corrupts that lock-control slot with data from slot 3. Because reglk_ctrl_o feeds acct_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, dma_wrapper, and others, a corrupted bit in reglk_mem[2] can selectively disable or incorrectly enable access guards on protected registers including access-control memory and cryptographic key readback paths.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can trigger a write to reglk address slot 2 while the lock bit is asserted can cause reglk_mem[2] to be loaded with the value of reglk_mem[3], potentially clearing lock bits that protect sensitive peripheral registers. This can bypass register-lock protection on acct_mem entries and allow unauthorized readback of pkey_loc or fuse data through pkt_wrapper.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact bit-field mapping of reglk_mem[2] to specific peripheral lock bits would require full NB_PERIPHERALS parameterization trace, but the structural defect in the guard logic is directly visible in the source snippet. The attack surface depends on whether the write path to reglk address 2 is accessible to unprivileged software.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper reset condition uses a logical OR of rst_ni negation and rst_6, meaning that asserting rst_6 alone (without deasserting rst_ni) resets all acct_mem entries to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-ones), which sets all access control permissions to fully open. This is an unsafe reset behavior: a secondary reset signal can force all access-control memory to the maximally permissive state.

evidence.description: The always block at line 79-112 uses the condition `if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6))` to trigger reset. This means asserting rst_6 (even while rst_ni is high) causes all acct_mem entries to be set to 32'hffffffff.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting rst_6 without deasserting rst_ni resets acct_mem to all-ones, which is the maximally permissive access control state.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is derived from acct_mem: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};`. When acct_mem is 0xFFFFFFFF, all access control output bits are set, granting maximal permissions to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem holds access control state that directly drives acc_ctrl_o; resetting it to all-ones grants all permissions.

evidence.description: rst_6 is declared as one of many numbered reset signals in riscv_peripherals.sv, suggesting it is a per-peripheral reset that can be independently asserted, potentially by software or an attacker with register access.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is a separate controllable reset signal, not tied to a full system reset, making it possible to trigger the unsafe all-ones reset of acct_mem independently.

evidence.description: The acct_wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals connects rst_6 and reglk_ctrl, showing rst_6 is an external input to the ACCT module that can be driven independently from the system reset rst_ni.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is wired as an independent input to acct_wrapper, confirming an attacker or misconfiguration can trigger the all-ones reset without a full system reset.

evidence.description: On reset (via rst_6), acct_mem is loaded with 32'hffffffff (line 84). The read-side logic (lines 117-143) returns acct_mem contents unless the reglk_ctrl bit is set. With all bits at 1, all guarded read paths return all-ones, granting maximum access. The write-side access control checks (lines 90-108) are also bypassed during the reset event itself.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset value of acct_mem (all-ones) is the maximally permissive state for access control, and rst_6 can trigger this without requiring a full system reset, violating the security intent of the access control memory.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl drives access permissions for all 14 peripherals (NB_PERIPHERALS=14). Setting all bits to 1 via acct_mem reset would grant unrestricted access to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The impact of the unsafe reset extends to all peripherals managed by the access control system.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper uses a combined reset condition `~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)` which is equivalent to `~rst_ni || rst_6`. This means either deasserting rst_ni OR asserting rst_6 triggers a reset of acct_mem to 32'hffffffff (all-ones). Since rst_6 is an independently controllable per-peripheral reset signal (not gated by any privilege or lock check in the snippets provided), an attacker or misconfiguration that asserts rst_6 will force all access control memory to the maximally permissive state (all bits = 1), bypassing the intended lock-based protection of peripheral access controls.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or privileged-but-not-fully-trusted entity that can assert rst_6 can reset all access control memory (acct_mem) to all-ones, granting unrestricted access to all 14 peripherals covered by the access control system. This completely defeats the purpose of the ACCT (Access Control Table) module and the reglk (register lock) protection scheme.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the provided snippets how rst_6 is driven (e.g., from a software-writable register or a hardware-only signal). If rst_6 is only asserted by trusted hardware, the exploitability is lower. However, the unsafe reset value (all-ones = fully permissive) is a clear design flaw regardless of the rst_6 drive source.

finding.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition includes jtag_unlock as a reset trigger, allowing JTAG to clear all register-lock (reglk_mem) values to zero, thereby removing all register protection locks across peripherals.

evidence.description: The always block reset condition is `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`, which evaluates to true (and clears all reglk_mem entries to 0) whenever jtag_unlock is asserted. This means asserting jtag_unlock zeroes out all register lock values, unconditionally removing all register protections.
evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock assertion clears all reglk_mem lock values to zero without any privilege or authentication check visible in the RTL snippet.

evidence.description: reglk_mem is the source of reglk_ctrl_o, which propagates to all peripheral wrappers (acct_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, dma_wrapper) as the register-lock control signal. Clearing reglk_mem to zero disables all register locks.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem drives the register-lock output bus; zeroing it removes all lock protections system-wide.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl output from reglk_wrapper is distributed to all peripheral wrappers including acct_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, and dma_wrapper. If reglk_mem is zeroed by jtag_unlock, all downstream guards become ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock control bus originates at reglk_wrapper and is distributed to all protected peripherals, so clearing it via jtag_unlock breaks all downstream access controls.

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, register writes and reads to acct_mem are gated by reglk_ctrl bits. If reglk_ctrl is all zeros (due to jtag_unlock clearing reglk_mem), all acct_mem entries become readable and writable.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control memory (acct_mem) protection relies entirely on reglk_ctrl values being non-zero; jtag_unlock-induced reset nullifies these protections.

evidence.description: dma_wrapper also receives reglk_ctrl from reglk_wrapper. With all lock bits zeroed, DMA configuration registers (start_reg, source/dest address registers) become unprotected and writable by any bus master.
evidence.supports_claim: Zeroing reglk_ctrl via jtag_unlock exposes DMA configuration to unauthorized modification, enabling arbitrary memory transfers.

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper's always block uses `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)` as its reset/clear condition. When jtag_unlock is asserted, the entire reglk_mem array is cleared to zero. This register memory is the sole source of the reglk_ctrl_o bus that gates read and write access to all peripheral registers (acct_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, dma_wrapper). Clearing it to zero disables all register-lock protections simultaneously. There is no authentication or privilege check guarding the jtag_unlock signal visible in the provided source snippets, making this a privilege bypass via the JTAG interface.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the JTAG interface can assert jtag_unlock to clear all register-lock protections across the system, enabling unauthorized reads and writes to access-control memory (acct_mem), DMA configuration registers, and other protected peripheral state. This can lead to privilege escalation, DMA-based memory exfiltration or corruption, and bypass of all software-configured register protections.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The authentication or privilege requirements for asserting jtag_unlock are not visible in the provided source snippets. If jtag_unlock requires a secure authentication handshake elsewhere in the design, the severity would be reduced, but the architectural pattern of using JTAG to clear security-critical lock registers remains a design concern.

finding.summary: The DMA wrapper's `en` signal used to gate register writes is derived solely from `en_acct && acct_ctrl_i`, but `acct_ctrl_i` is connected from `acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8]` — a value produced by the ACCT module whose `acc_ctrl_o` output includes an unconditional OR with `{8{we_flag}}`. evidence.description: acct_wrapper write side guards every acct_mem write with a reglk_ctrl bit check (e.g., reglk_ctrl[5] ? acct_mem[00] : wdata), further confirming the intended lock pattern that is missing in pkt_wrapper for fuse_addr_o and fuse_req_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Correct guard pattern exists in acct_wrapper but is absent in pkt_wrapper for fuse registers.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with reglk_ctrl_i bound to reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8], confirming that a lock byte is specifically allocated for PKT but the write side of the module does not honor it for fuse_addr_o and fuse_req_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Lock bits are provisioned for pkt_wrapper at the system level but not enforced internally for fuse address writes.

evidence.description: fuse_addr_o is declared as an output from pkt_wrapper driving the fuse memory address bus. Unguarded writes to this signal allow an attacker to redirect fuse read requests to arbitrary fuse addresses, enabling unauthorized readback of sensitive fuse data via fuse_rdata_i.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_addr_o controls access to fuse memory; unguarded write allows information leakage from arbitrary fuse locations.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper module receives a reglk_ctrl_i lock byte and uses it to gate reads of sensitive registers (pkey_loc, fuse_rdata_i) in the read-side always block. However, the write-side always block unconditionally updates fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o when 'en && we' is asserted, with no reglk_ctrl_i check. Every comparable peripheral (acct_wrapper, reglk_wrapper) applies lock checks on their write paths. This omission allows software with write access to pkt_wrapper to override the fuse memory address pointer and request signal even when the lock register is set, potentially redirecting fuse reads to attacker-controlled addresses and leaking sensitive fuse data.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI write access to the PKT peripheral can freely program fuse_addr_o to point to any fuse memory location and assert fuse_req_o, then read the result back through fuse_rdata_i, bypassing the intended register-lock protection. This enables unauthorized readback of secret fuse values (e.g., cryptographic keys, device identity bits) stored at locations not otherwise accessible.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to access the PKT AXI slave is not fully visible from the snippets provided (depends on PMP/access-control configuration in riscv_peripherals). However, the structural violation—missing lock check on write path while read path has it—is unambiguous from the source code.

finding.summary: fuse_rdata_i (sensitive fuse/key data) is returned to the bus when en=1 as a default before the case statement, and is explicitly readable at address case 4 only when reglk_ctrl_i[6]=0. However, the default branch at line 96 assigns fuse_rdata_i to rdata unconditionally (before the case overrides it), meaning any unmatched address while en=1 leaks fuse data. Additionally, the fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o registers (controlling fuse memory access) are writable without any reglk_ctrl_i lock check.

evidence.description: fuse_rdata_i carries sensitive fuse/key data read from the fuse memory. It is declared as an input on line 32 of pkt_wrapper.sv and flows into the rdata output bus.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_rdata_i is a sensitive resource that should be protected from unauthorized readback.

evidence.description: At line 103, reading pkey_loc[63:32] is gated by reglk_ctrl_i[4]; at line 105, pkey_loc[31:0] is gated by reglk_ctrl_i[5]; at line 107, fuse_rdata_i is gated by reglk_ctrl_i[6]. These are the only reglk-based read guards present.
evidence.supports_claim: There is a partial lock mechanism for specific address cases, but it is incomplete.

evidence.description: At line 96, 'rdata = fuse_rdata_i' is assigned unconditionally as the first statement inside 'if (en)', before the case statement. This means for any address that is not handled by cases 0-4 AND does not satisfy the 'fuse_addr_o <= 110' default sub-condition, the rdata output will hold fuse_rdata_i. More critically, even for addresses that ARE handled by the case, rdata is first set to fuse_rdata_i and then overwritten — but for any unrecognized address outside the explicit cases, fuse_rdata_i leaks out as the returned value.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive fuse data (fuse_rdata_i) is leaked via rdata for unmatched addresses when en=1, bypassing the reglk_ctrl_i[6] lock that only protects case 4.

evidence.description: At lines 83-85, fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o (which control what fuse memory address is read) are written when 'en && we' without any reglk_ctrl_i lock check on the write path. An attacker with en=1 and we=1 can freely program fuse memory access requests.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing write-side reglk_ctrl_i guard allows unauthorized modification of fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o.

evidence.description: Line 68: 'assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i;' — access is gated by acct_ctrl_i (access control) and the AXI enable signal. Once en=1, read and write operations proceed subject only to the case-specific reglk checks.
evidence.supports_claim: The en signal is the primary access gate; once bypassed or legitimately asserted, incomplete downstream guards allow sensitive data leakage.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o is built from reglk_mem registers and distributed to all peripherals including pkt_wrapper. The lock bits reglk_ctrl_i[6:4] in pkt_wrapper gate only specific read cases (2, 3, 4) but not the default fuse_rdata_i assignment at line 96.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk system provides partial protection but the pkt_wrapper implementation does not correctly apply it to all paths where fuse_rdata_i can be returned.

evidence.description: fuse_rdata (the external fuse memory read data) is wired directly into pkt_wrapper as fuse_rdata_i. This data represents physical fuse values which typically contain secret keys or device identity information. Leakage of this data via unguarded rdata paths constitutes a sensitive readback vulnerability.
evidence.supports_claim: Exploitation allows software with AXI access to pkt_wrapper to read raw fuse memory contents beyond what the lock bits were intended to permit.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper module receives sensitive fuse memory read data via fuse_rdata_i. The read-side logic (lines 92-113 in SNIP-0011) first unconditionally assigns rdata = fuse_rdata_i when en=1 (line 96), then uses a case statement to override rdata for specific addresses. Case 4 (address[7:3]==4) correctly gates fuse_rdata_i behind reglk_ctrl_i[6], but the initial assignment at line 96 means any address not falling into cases 0-4 (or not satisfying the default sub-condition) will leak fuse_rdata_i. Furthermore, the write path (lines 80-88) for fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o has no reglk_ctrl_i lock check, allowing unrestricted programming of fuse memory access requests when en && we is true.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access to the pkt_wrapper peripheral (subject to acct_ctrl_i being asserted) can: (1) read raw fuse memory data by accessing unhandled register addresses, bypassing the reglk_ctrl_i[6] lock intended to protect fuse readback; (2) freely write fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o to control which fuse addresses are fetched, with no lock protection on the write path. Fuse memory typically contains device secret keys or provisioning data, so this constitutes a high-severity sensitive data disclosure.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact content of fuse memory (whether it contains cryptographic keys or less sensitive data) is not confirmed from the snippets alone, but the naming convention and circuit structure strongly imply sensitive security fuse data. The default case behavior at line 109 ('if (fuse_addr_o <= 110) rdata = 32'b0') partially mitigates the leakage for some address ranges, but the condition depends on fuse_addr_o which is attacker-controllable, reducing this mitigation's effectiveness.

finding.summary: In pkt_wrapper, sensitive fuse data (fuse_rdata_i) and internal control signals (fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, pkey_loc) are readable without complete register-lock protection on all paths. Specifically, address cases 0 and 1 in the read path return fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o with no reglk_ctrl_i guard at all, while address case 4 returns raw fuse_rdata_i only gated by reglk_ctrl_i[6]. Additionally, the write path that drives fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o is gated only by the acct_ctrl_i/en signal with no reglk_ctrl_i check, allowing an authorized-but-unprivileged caller to write fuse interface control registers.

evidence.description: fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, pkey_loc, and fuse_rdata_i are the sensitive fuse-interface outputs defined and driven within pkt_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: These are the resources that should be protected by register-lock and access-control guards.

evidence.description: Read path (lines 92-113): address cases 0 and 1 return fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o respectively with NO reglk_ctrl_i check. Cases 2 and 3 check reglk_ctrl_i[4] and reglk_ctrl_i[5] for pkey_loc. Case 4 checks reglk_ctrl_i[6] for fuse_rdata_i. The outer 'if (en)' only checks access control, not register lock.
evidence.supports_claim: Guard logic is absent for address cases 0 and 1 in the read path, exposing fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o without lock protection.

evidence.description: Lines 94-101: 'rdata = fuse_rdata_i' is the default assignment inside 'if (en)', then case 0 sets rdata = {31'b0, fuse_req_o} and case 1 sets rdata = fuse_addr_o — neither branch checks any bit of reglk_ctrl_i, so these fuse control values are always readable when en is asserted regardless of lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing reglk_ctrl_i guard on read cases 0 and 1 allows fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o to be read without authorization lock check.

evidence.description: Write path (lines 73-89): fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o are written (cases 0 and 1) whenever 'en && we' is true. There is no reglk_ctrl_i guard on these write cases, unlike the acct_wrapper where reglk_ctrl bits are checked on every write case. An attacker with acct_ctrl_i permission can freely write fuse interface control signals.
evidence.supports_claim: Write path to fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o lacks reglk_ctrl_i protection, allowing unauthorized modification of fuse interface controls.

evidence.description: Line 68: 'assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i' — the only gate for enabling register access is the AXI interface enable ANDed with acct_ctrl_i. No register-lock bits are checked at this level.
evidence.supports_claim: Access to the pkt_wrapper register interface is gated solely by acct_ctrl_i, with no integration of reglk_ctrl_i into the enable path.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper write path (lines 87-111) checks reglk_ctrl bits on every write case, demonstrating the expected pattern. pkt_wrapper's write path omits this pattern for fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The absence of reglk_ctrl_i guards on pkt_wrapper write cases 0 and 1 is inconsistent with the protection pattern used in sibling modules.

evidence.description: fuse_req and fuse_addr are wired directly to the fuse memory interface in riscv_peripherals (lines 1613-1614), so an attacker manipulating fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o can directly influence which fuse memory location is accessed.
evidence.supports_claim: Unguarded writes to fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o propagate directly to the physical fuse memory read interface, enabling unauthorized fuse access.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper module exposes a fuse-memory access interface (fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, fuse_rdata_i). The read path returns fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o at address cases 0 and 1 without any reglk_ctrl_i guard (unlike cases 2, 3, and 4 which do check reglk_ctrl_i bits). The write path stores values into fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o whenever 'en && we' is true, without any reglk_ctrl_i check, contrary to the pattern used in acct_wrapper and reglk_wrapper. This allows any caller with acct_ctrl_i permission to both read and write the fuse interface control registers regardless of whether the register-lock mechanism has locked those registers.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who obtains acct_ctrl_i access (access-control permission) can: (1) read the current fuse request state and address without needing register-lock bypass; (2) arbitrarily write fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o to redirect fuse memory reads to any address, potentially exfiltrating secret fuse data (e.g., cryptographic keys stored in fuse memory) by polling fuse_rdata_i after redirecting the address.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet does not show the full fuse memory implementation or whether additional hardware enforces address bounds on fuse reads. The exact sensitivity of data at each fuse address is also not shown. However, the missing guard condition is directly visible in source code.

finding.summary: reglk_wrapper resets all lock registers to 0 (unlocked) on jtag_unlock assertion, allowing JTAG to bypass all register lock protections for PKT, ACCT, and DMA peripherals

evidence.description: The reset condition in reglk_wrapper is: `if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9))`. When jtag_unlock is asserted, the condition evaluates true, resetting all reglk_mem entries to 0 (fully unlocked). This means any JTAG unlock signal clears all peripheral lock bits.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG unlock signal forces all lock registers to zero, removing all protections simultaneously

evidence.description: At line 83, `reglk_mem[j] <= 'h0` is assigned for all 6 entries when the reset condition is true. Since jtag_unlock is part of that condition (line 80), asserting jtag_unlock resets ALL lock bits to 0 (unlocked state), bypassing protections for all peripherals including PKT, ACCT, and DMA.
evidence.supports_claim: All register lock protections are simultaneously cleared to unlocked state by jtag_unlock, which is an overly broad JTAG debug bypass

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o is directly driven by reglk_mem contents: `assign reglk_ctrl_o = {reglk_mem[5], reglk_mem[4], reglk_mem[3], reglk_mem[2], reglk_mem[1], reglk_mem[0]}`. When reglk_mem is zeroed by jtag_unlock, reglk_ctrl_o becomes all zeros.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock control output to all peripherals is derived directly from reglk_mem, so zeroing reglk_mem propagates unlocked state to all protected peripherals

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl output from reglk_wrapper feeds directly into riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl, which is then distributed to pkt_wrapper, acct_wrapper, and dma_wrapper as their reglk_ctrl_i inputs.
evidence.supports_claim: The zeroed lock state propagates to all peripheral wrappers simultaneously

evidence.description: In pkt_wrapper, sensitive registers (pkey_loc at addresses 2-3, fuse_rdata at address 4) are read-protected by reglk_ctrl_i bits [4], [5], [6]. When these bits are 0 (after jtag_unlock), sensitive data like pkey_loc and fuse data become readable.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material (pkey_loc, fuse data) in PKT wrapper becomes readable when lock bits are cleared by jtag_unlock

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, access control memory entries are read-protected by reglk_ctrl bits [4], [2], [0], [6]. When these are 0, all acct_mem contents are readable and writable (write protection also disabled).
evidence.supports_claim: Access control policy table in ACCT wrapper becomes fully readable and writable when lock bits are cleared

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module manages lock bits for all peripherals. Its reset logic at line 80 includes `~jtag_unlock` in the reset condition, meaning when jtag_unlock is asserted, all 6 reglk_mem registers are reset to 0 (unlocked). This zeroed state flows through reglk_ctrl_o to riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl, which is distributed to pkt_wrapper, acct_wrapper, and dma_wrapper. With all lock bits at 0, pkt_wrapper exposes sensitive key data (pkey_loc) and fuse read data, and acct_wrapper exposes and allows modification of access control policy. This represents an unsafe debug/lifecycle behavior where a single JTAG signal bypasses all security locks across all peripherals.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can assert jtag_unlock to simultaneously clear all register lock protections across all peripherals, enabling read-back of sensitive key material (pkey_loc, fuse data) and unauthorized modification of access control policies that govern all peripheral access rights.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is unclear whether jtag_unlock itself is protected by authentication before it can be asserted. If jtag_unlock requires prior authentication, the severity is reduced. The source snippets do not show the jtag_unlock source or its authentication chain.

finding.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition includes jtag_unlock as a reset trigger, allowing JTAG to clear all register-lock memory (reglk_mem) to zero, which disables all peripheral access controls and enables unrestricted access to protected peripherals.

evidence.description: The reset condition in reglk_wrapper is `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`. When jtag_unlock is asserted, this condition evaluates true and forces all reglk_mem entries to 0x0, clearing all register-lock bits.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG can clear all register-lock state, bypassing the lock protection mechanism.

evidence.description: reglk_mem is a 6-element array of 32-bit lock registers exported as reglk_ctrl_o and used to gate read/write access across all peripherals. Zeroing it removes all locks.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem drives all peripheral lock controls; clearing it via JTAG effectively unlocks every protected register.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper read/write paths are gated by reglk_ctrl bits (reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[13], reglk_ctrl[1], reglk_ctrl[7], reglk_ctrl[4], reglk_ctrl[2], reglk_ctrl[0], reglk_ctrl[6]). When reglk_mem is zeroed, all these guards become 0 (unlocked), exposing acct_mem to unauthorized access.
evidence.supports_claim: All access-control memory in acct_wrapper becomes readable and writable when JTAG clears reglk_mem.

evidence.description: dma_wrapper registers (start_reg, length_reg, source/dest addr regs, core_lock_reg) are gated by en which depends on acct_ctrl_i and reglk state. If JTAG clears reglk_mem, DMA registers become accessible to any privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: DMA configuration registers are exposed when lock state is cleared by JTAG.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper is instantiated with jtag_unlock connected from riscv_peripherals, confirming the JTAG unlock signal is live and wired to reglk_wrapper's reset-clearing logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The JTAG unlock path is architecturally present and connected, making the reset bypass exploitable.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl and distributed to all peripheral wrappers (acct, reglk, dma, pkt) as the access control input. If JTAG clears reglk_mem and thereby changes what is exported as reglk_ctrl_o, it also affects the lock bits used by acc_ctrl in acct_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The acc_ctrl_c distribution confirms that clearing reglk_mem has downstream effects on the entire permission chain.

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module holds all peripheral register-lock state in reglk_mem. Its reset/clear condition (`~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`) means that asserting jtag_unlock forces every reglk_mem entry to 0x0. Since reglk_mem drives reglk_ctrl_o which is used as lock guards in acct_wrapper, dma_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, and reglk_wrapper itself, clearing it via JTAG removes all protection on reading and writing security-critical registers. This is confirmed by the source code: SNIP-0016 shows the JTAG-triggered reset sets all reglk_mem to 0, and SNIP-0010/SNIP-0013 show those bits directly gate access to acct_mem and DMA registers.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can assert jtag_unlock to zero all lock registers, then freely read or overwrite access-control memory (acct_mem) and DMA configuration registers regardless of privilege level. This completely defeats the hardware access-control enforcement layer, enabling privilege escalation, DMA-based arbitrary memory access, and disclosure of sensitive key/fuse data.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact condition under which jtag_unlock can be asserted by an adversary (e.g., whether it is gated by authentication) is not shown in the provided snippets. However, the structural vulnerability—that asserting jtag_unlock clears all lock state—is clearly evidenced in the source code.

finding.summary: AXI protection bits (aw.prot and ar.prot) are hardwired to zero when driving the reglk_wrapper, eliminating any privilege/security-level enforcement on accesses to the register-lock peripheral.

evidence.description: Lines 1874 and 1885 unconditionally assign reglk_axi_req.aw.prot and reglk_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (unprivileged, unsecure, data access). All other AXI control fields that are unused by AXI-lite are also tied off, but the prot field is security-critical. By hardwiring prot=0, any requester that reaches the reglk_wrapper appears to the peripheral as an unprivileged, non-secure data transaction regardless of the actual privilege level of the originating master.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields that carry privilege and Secure/Non-Secure world information are permanently forced to 3'b000, bypassing any protection-state check that reglk_wrapper or a downstream interconnect might perform based on those bits.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper exposes reglk_ctrl_o which carries register-lock values governing access to NB_PERIPHERALS peripherals, and accepts reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i from callers. The peripheral is explicitly a security asset; unauthorized writes to it could unlock registers that are supposed to remain locked.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_wrapper is the register-lock controller for the SoC peripherals; any bypass of its access controls directly affects the integrity of peripheral register protection.

evidence.description: The reglk_wrapper is instantiated with its axi_req_i port connected to reglk_axi_req, which is the same signal whose prot bits are hardwired to zero. No privilege filtering is applied before this connection.
evidence.supports_claim: The request path from an AXI-lite master into reglk_wrapper carries no protection-level information, making it impossible for the peripheral to distinguish privileged from unprivileged accesses.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0002 shows that reglk_axi_req.aw.prot and reglk_axi_req.ar.prot are both tied to '0 as part of the AXI-lite tie-off block. AXI PROT[0] distinguishes privileged from unprivileged accesses, and PROT[1] distinguishes Secure from Non-Secure world. Forcing these to zero means that (a) the reglk_wrapper cannot reject unprivileged or non-secure accesses based on the prot field, and (b) if any downstream access-control logic uses prot to gate writes, that logic is rendered ineffective. Given that reglk_wrapper controls register-lock bits for the entire peripheral subsystem (SNIP-0003), this represents a missing guard on a security-critical peripheral.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure master that can reach the AXI-lite adapter feeding reglk_wrapper will appear to the peripheral as if it were making a legitimate privileged/secure access because prot is always 0. This could allow unauthorized modification of register-lock control bits, potentially unlocking registers that protect sensitive peripherals across the SoC.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The reglk_wrapper internals (beyond its port list) are not fully shown, so it is uncertain whether reglk_wrapper itself checks prot internally or relies on upstream interconnect filtering. Additionally, the AXI-lite adapter that populates the non-tied fields of reglk_axi_req is not shown, so it is unclear whether the adapter propagates a real prot signal that is then overridden by these assigns or whether prot was never connected. If the upstream interconnect already enforces privilege gating before this point, the impact is reduced.

Insufficient source evidence exists to support a concrete permission-related vulnerability finding.

finding.summary: jtag_unlock signal can reset reglk_mem lock registers to zero, effectively bypassing all register lock protections by asserting a JTAG debug signal

Asserting jtag_unlock causes all lock registers reglk_mem[0..5] to be zeroed, which disables all register lock protections controlled by reglk_ctrl.
evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock directly clears security lock registers, bypassing all peripheral write protections

evidence.description: reglk_mem is the backing storage for reglk_ctrl_o, which controls write-lock bits for all peripherals. Zeroing reglk_mem removes all lock protections.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem holds the permission state bits that protect peripheral registers; clearing it removes all access restrictions

evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock is a JTAG/debug-driven signal that can override the lock state of security registers

evidence.description: At line 1817, jtag_unlock is connected from riscv_peripherals into reglk_wrapper's jtag_unlock port, confirming the signal flows from a top-level debug interface into the register lock module.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_unlock signal originates externally and is wired directly to the lock register reset path

evidence.description: Normal writes to reglk_mem are guarded by reglk_ctrl bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl[3], reglk_ctrl[1]) at lines 89-99. However, the reset path triggered by jtag_unlock at line 80 bypasses all these guards and unconditionally zeros all lock registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_unlock reset path has no authentication or privilege guard — it unconditionally clears all lock bits

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, write access to acct_mem entries is guarded by reglk_ctrl bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[13], reglk_ctrl[1], reglk_ctrl[7]). If reglk_mem is zeroed via jtag_unlock, all these guards become 0, allowing unrestricted writes to access control memory.
evidence.supports_claim: Clearing reglk_mem via jtag_unlock removes all write protections on access control registers across the system

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module implements register lock controls for all peripherals. Its reglk_mem array is reset to zero when the condition `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)` is true, which triggers on assertion of jtag_unlock. When reglk_mem is zeroed, all peripheral write-lock bits (reglk_ctrl_o) become 0, disabling all register-level access protections. This means any attacker or debug interface capable of asserting jtag_unlock can bypass the entire register lock security mechanism. There is no authentication or privilege level check on the jtag_unlock signal before it is allowed to clear security state. The impact extends to acct_wrapper (access control registers), pkt_wrapper, and dma_wrapper, all of which rely on reglk_ctrl for write protection.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or privileged debug entity that can assert jtag_unlock can clear all register lock protections (reglk_mem → 0), enabling unrestricted modification of access control registers (acct_mem), PKT fuse registers, DMA control registers, and all other peripherals protected by the reglk_ctrl mechanism. This constitutes a complete bypass of the hardware security policy enforced by the register lock subsystem.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of the jtag_unlock signal (how it is set/cleared and what authentication, if any, precedes its assertion) is not shown in the provided snippets. If jtag_unlock is gated by a proper authentication mechanism upstream, the severity could be reduced. However, based on available code evidence, no such guard is visible within the chain.

finding.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition includes jtag_unlock, allowing JTAG to clear all register-lock bits (reglk_mem) to zero, effectively removing all peripheral access protections at runtime. Additionally, the DMA wrapper write path lacks any reglk_ctrl guard, unlike other peripherals.

evidence.description: The reset condition for reglk_mem is `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`. When jtag_unlock is asserted, all reglk_mem entries are cleared to 0x0, which disables all register locks across all peripherals. This gives JTAG the ability to unilaterally clear the lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG unlock signal can trigger a full reset of all register-lock control bits, bypassing the intended locked state of peripheral registers.

evidence.description: reglk_mem holds all register lock values output as reglk_ctrl_o. Clearing reglk_mem to 0 via jtag_unlock removes all protections simultaneously, as the output is a direct concatenation of reglk_mem entries.
evidence.supports_claim: All peripheral lock states are stored in reglk_mem; zeroing it disables all locks.

evidence.description: The DMA wrapper write-side always block does not check reglk_ctrl_i before updating any of its registers (start_reg, length_reg, source_addr, dest_addr, done_reg, core_lock_reg, end_reg). finding.summary: AXI protection bits (aw.prot and ar.prot) for the AES0 peripheral are hard-wired to zero, disabling all AXI privilege, security, and data/instruction access protection on every transaction issued to the AES crypto accelerator.

evidence.description: Line 1250 assigns aes0_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and line 1261 assigns aes0_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. In the AXI4 protocol the PROT field encodes privileged/unprivileged, secure/non-secure, and instruction/data attributes. Forcing both to zero means every transaction is presented as unprivileged, secure, and data-type, regardless of the actual initiator privilege level. No conditional guard or runtime check is applied; these are unconditional continuous assignments.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields are permanently zeroed, eliminating the AXI security/privilege signalling that downstream interconnect, memory protection units, or firewall logic may rely on to enforce access control to the AES peripheral and its key/data registers.

evidence.description: The target of these AXI transactions is the AES0 cryptographic accelerator (aes0_axi_req). AES peripherals typically hold or process sensitive key material and plaintext/ciphertext. Correct PROT encoding is essential for interconnect-level firewalls to restrict access to privileged or secure-world initiators only.

evidence.description: The comment on line 1243 states these are 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite', implying the design intentionally discards the PROT field because the upstream adapter is AXI-lite. However, the downstream port is full AXI4 (ariane_axi::req_t), so the PROT value propagates to any downstream firewall or memory protection logic that inspects it.

finding.reasoning_summary: The AXI4 PROT sideband field is used by interconnect components, TrustZone-aware firewalls, and memory protection units to enforce privilege and security domain separation. Lines 1250 and 1261 unconditionally assign aw.prot and ar.prot to zero for all AES0 transactions. This means every read and write to the AES0 peripheral will appear to be an unprivileged, secure, data-access transaction regardless of the true privilege level of the initiating core. If any downstream firewall or bus matrix relies on these bits to restrict AES key register access to privileged or secure-world software only, that protection is silently bypassed for every transaction.
finding.security_impact: Unprivileged or non-secure software may be able to read or write AES key registers and data registers through the AXI bus if downstream firewalls use aw.prot/ar.prot for access control, because those fields are always presented as the least-restricted value (0). This can lead to key extraction, key injection, or ciphertext manipulation by unprivileged attackers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether any downstream bus firewall or memory protection unit actually uses the PROT field for enforcement is not visible in this chain context. If the interconnect ignores PROT entirely, there is no exploitable path through this specific signal. Additionally, the comment suggests this is a known AXI-lite adapter limitation; a proper fix would require propagating PROT from the CPU's privilege level through the adapter.

finding.summary: AXI protection bits (aw.prot and ar.prot) for AES1 peripheral are permanently tied to zero, disabling all AXI privilege, security, and instruction/data access type signaling.

evidence.description: Line 1336 assigns aes1_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and line 1347 assigns aes1_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. The AXI4 PROT field encodes three bits: [0] privileged access, [1] non-secure access, [2] instruction access. Setting all bits to zero means every AXI transaction to the AES1 peripheral is presented as unprivileged, secure, and data-type, regardless of the actual initiator privilege level. Downstream slaves or interconnect firewalls that rely on PROT to enforce access control will see all requests as having identical, lowest-privilege attributes, bypassing any protection checks based on those signals.
evidence.supports_claim: aw.prot and ar.prot are hardwired to 3'b000, permanently suppressing privilege and security-level signaling on all AES1 AXI transactions.

evidence.description: The target of the AXI requests is the AES1 peripheral (a cryptographic accelerator). AXI transactions carrying key material or plaintext/ciphertext use these channels. evidence.description: Lines 1334-1335 (aw.lock, aw.cache) and 1344-1346 (ar.lock, ar.cache) are also tied to zero, but more critically the comment 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite' explains the intent. finding.reasoning_summary: The AXI4 PROT signal is a standard mechanism for communicating access privilege level (privileged/unprivileged), security domain (secure/non-secure), and access type (instruction/data) from an initiator to a target or interconnect firewall. In this design, both the write-channel (aw.prot) and read-channel (ar.prot) protection bits for the AES1 AXI interface are hardwired to all-zeros. This means every transaction reaches the AES1 peripheral (or any intervening bus protection unit) appearing as unprivileged, secure, and data-type, irrespective of the true privilege level of the CPU core issuing the request. Any firewall or slave-side access control that keys on PROT bits to grant or deny access is effectively bypassed—unprivileged software could read or write AES key registers if the only guard was a PROT-based check, and privileged accesses lose their distinguishing signal.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software agent that should be denied access to the AES1 peripheral's key or control registers may gain access because all AXI transactions present identical unprivileged-looking PROT bits. Conversely, security monitors or TrustZone-style firewalls that rely on the non-secure bit (PROT[1]) to route or block transactions cannot distinguish secure from non-secure initiators, potentially allowing non-secure world software to reach AES cryptographic resources.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream firewall or slave access-control policy for AES1 is not visible in this chain; if no downstream component uses PROT bits for filtering, the practical impact is limited. Also, the AXI-lite bridge upstream may itself recompute or override PROT before this tie-off point, which would reduce severity. Full confirmation requires inspecting the AES1 slave and any interconnect access-control IP.

evidence.description: Lines 1421 and 1432 unconditionally assign aes2_axi_req.aw.prot and aes2_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (3'b000). A downstream interconnect or memory controller that enforces access control based on AWPROT/ARPROT will therefore treat AES2 accesses as untrusted/unprivileged, or — depending on firewall polarity — may allow them through a path that was intended only for secure/privileged masters.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that aes2_axi_req.aw.prot and aes2_axi_req.ar.prot are both statically assigned to 3'b000 as part of a bulk tie-off of AXI-lite unused signals. The AXI PROT field is the standard mechanism downstream firewalls and TrustZone-aware interconnects use to enforce privilege and secure/non-secure separation. Hardcoding it to zero permanently marks all AES2 transactions as unprivileged non-secure data accesses. This means: (1) a firewall configured to allow only secure-world access to the AES peripheral will incorrectly block legitimate secure-world requests, or (2) a firewall configured to block non-secure access may be misconfigured because it never sees the correct security attribute, creating an exploitable path. The AES2 peripheral handles cryptographic operations where incorrect privilege/security signaling directly undermines hardware-enforced isolation.
finding.security_impact: All transactions to the AES2 cryptographic peripheral are issued with PROT=3'b000 (unprivileged, non-secure, data), regardless of the actual CPU privilege or TrustZone security state. Any downstream firewall or memory protection unit relying on AXI PROT bits for access control will make incorrect access decisions, potentially allowing unprivileged software to access AES key registers or blocking legitimate secure-world use. This undermines hardware-enforced isolation of the AES accelerator.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream firewall/interconnect configuration is not in scope, so it is unknown whether any bus protection unit actually enforces PROT-based filtering for this path. If the SoC uses no PROT-based filtering at all, the practical exploitability is lower. Additionally, the AXI-lite bridge upstream may itself override PROT before the fabric, which would change the impact. The snippet does not show the full AXI-lite to AXI4 bridge or whether the bridge propagates a separate PROT input.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The source snippet shows standard AXI-lite adapter tie-off assignments for the CLINT AXI request bus. Fields such as aw.id, aw.len, aw.burst, aw.lock, aw.cache, aw.prot, ar.lock, ar.cache, ar.prot, etc. are set to '0 as required when bridging a full AXI bus to an AXI-lite peripheral that does not use those fields. The aw.prot and ar.prot fields being tied to '0 is the standard AXI-lite tie-off pattern for internal bus adapters and does not constitute a bypassed privilege or protection check in this context — CLINT is an internal machine-mode peripheral accessed only by the RISC-V core itself. No missing guard, bypassed lock, unsafe reset behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update is evidenced in the provided source snippets. The chain contains only structural wiring assignments with no permission logic to violate.

Its output debug_req_bits_op is directly wired to dmi_req.op through a simple type cast with no authentication, lock, or lifecycle permission check. finding.summary: The pkt_wrapper exposes fuse_addr and fuse_req registers without reglk_ctrl protection on writes, and allows sensitive fuse readback (fuse_rdata_i) at address case 4 only gated by reglk_ctrl_i[6], while fuse_addr_o and fuse_req_o are readable at addresses 0 and 1 with no lock check at all.

The default case (line 109-110) also returns 32'b0 only when fuse_addr_o <= 110, otherwise implicitly returns fuse_rdata_i set at line 96.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl[5*8+7:5*8] is passed to pkt_wrapper as reglk_ctrl_i. The reglk_ctrl bus is produced by reglk_wrapper and distributed to all peripherals. evidence.description: reglk_wrapper outputs reglk_ctrl_o which is wired back into riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl and distributed to pkt_wrapper, acct_wrapper, and dma_wrapper. This is the authoritative lock state for all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Lock state is properly generated and distributed, confirming the missing guards in pkt_wrapper are implementation omissions, not architectural gaps.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper implements a register-lock scheme via reglk_ctrl_i but applies it inconsistently. pkey_loc and fuse_rdata_i readbacks are gated by reglk_ctrl_i bits [4], [5], [6] respectively. However, fuse_req_o (address 0) and fuse_addr_o (address 1) are readable with no lock check. finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI access (passing acct_ctrl_i check) can read back the programmed fuse address and request state, and can also obtain raw fuse memory data via the default read-path bypass of reglk_ctrl_i[6]. They can also freely write fuse_req and fuse_addr after the lock has been set for other resources, potentially redirecting fuse reads to arbitrary addresses.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of pkey_loc and the full fuse memory access model are not shown. The reglk_wrapper internal implementation (which bits map to which protections) is not fully visible, but the pkt_wrapper source code directly demonstrates the missing guards.

finding.summary: fuse_rdata (sensitive fuse data) is readable without a complete register-lock guard: when the register-lock bit reglk_ctrl_i[6] is clear the raw fuse_rdata_i is returned at address case 4, but at line 96 fuse_rdata_i is also unconditionally assigned to rdata before the case statement is evaluated, and several case branches (0, 1, default) return non-locked data while the default branch at line 109-110 only conditionally returns zero without checking any lock bit.

finding.summary: fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o registers in pkt_wrapper are readable without a register-lock guard, while fuse_rdata_i (the actual fuse data) is gated by reglk_ctrl_i[6]. However, fuse_req_o is directly readable at address 0 and fuse_addr_o at address 1 with no reglk_ctrl_i check, potentially leaking the fuse access request state and address to any unprivileged accessor.

evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o readback constitutes a permission bypass for sensitive security-relevant signals.

evidence.supports_claim: The lock control bus has unused protection bits that should guard fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o readback paths.

evidence.description: fuse_req and fuse_addr are wired from pkt_wrapper outputs in riscv_peripherals, making them accessible as part of the PKT peripheral AXI slave interface exposed to software.
evidence.supports_claim: The unguarded readback paths are reachable via the AXI interface mapped into the software-accessible peripheral address space.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper module implements a register-lock mechanism via reglk_ctrl_i for sensitive fields (pkey_loc, fuse_rdata_i) but omits equivalent guards for fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o in the read path. Since the 'en' signal gates the entire read block, any accessor with AXI access to the PKT peripheral can read the fuse request status and target address without privilege restriction. This is an inconsistent protection state that exposes security-sensitive signals.
